serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of lamb seekh kebab consists of 2–3 skewers weighing approximately 120 g, often accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Sheekh Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010853 is sourced directly from HPB Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are preserved as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat kebab
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb seekh kebab. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 5.24 g/100g; GL per 120 g serving = (30 × 5.24 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 1.9, rounded to 2.0. Estimation is conservative and consistent with the low-carbohydrate, high-fat, high-protein profile of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — critically elevated, well above the 600 mg/100g disqualification threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or severely restrict lamb seekh kebab due to its critically high sodium content of 1080 mg/100g; if consumed, limit to a very small portion (≤50 g) and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as chutneys or pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low GI support glycaemic stability, but high fat (26.4 g/100g) and high sodium (1080 mg/100g) are concerns for long-term metabolic health
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb seekh kebab has a very low carbohydrate load and low glycaemic index, making it acceptable for blood glucose management in small portions, but the high saturated fat content may contribute to insulin resistance and the high sodium warrants caution in individuals with diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~10.8 g/100g), high cholesterol (85 mg/100g), and critically high sodium (1080 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb seekh kebab poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its combination of elevated saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume it only occasionally and in small portions, paired with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the lipid and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory); sodium 1080 mg/100g worsens tissue swelling; nightshade spices (green chilli, chilli powder) may trigger joint flares
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid lamb seekh kebab due to its high arachidonic acid content from lamb, critically high sodium, and the presence of nightshade-derived spices that may exacerbate joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Seekh Kebab is a popular South Asian grilled dish made from finely minced lamb mixed with aromatic spices including garam masala, chilli powder, ginger, garlic, green chilli, and fresh coriander. The seasoned mince is moulded around a flat metal or bamboo skewer and roasted over a charcoal grill or in a tandoor oven until cooked through with a lightly charred exterior. The result is a juicy, smoky, and intensely spiced kebab with a firm yet tender texture. In Singapore, lamb seekh kebab is commonly served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as a starter or main course, accompanied by mint chutney, sliced raw onion, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, the dish is high in fat (26.4 g/100g) and moderate in protein (10.7 g/100g) with very low carbohydrates (5.24 g/100g), yielding 302 kcal per 100g. Sodium is critically high at 1080 mg/100g, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index make it relatively safe for blood glucose management in small portions, but the high saturated fat and sodium require caution for individuals with metabolic or cardiovascular conditions. Dietary fibre is absent, so pairing with a high-fibre side dish is recommended.

notes: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — lamb seekh kebab contains no plant-based fibre-contributing ingredients in significant quantity. saturated_fat_g (10.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb mince cooked data (NDB 17066) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is an adapted value. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish entry as of 2026.
